explanation: The formula requires that whenever prop_0 holds at any moment, prop_1 must also hold at that same moment. Globally, at all points in time, if prop_0 is true, then prop_1 must also be true.
This formula expresses that globally, whenever prop_0 holds, then prop_1 must also hold at the same moment—in other words, prop_1 is required to be true at all times when prop_0 is true.
The natural language specifies that everywhere in the execution, if prop_0 is true at a time, then prop_1 must also be true at that time. This can be represented as 'globally, prop_0 implies prop_1'.
This LTL formula expresses that globally (at all times), if prop_0 holds, then prop_1 must also hold. It directly corresponds to the stated conditional.
The specification says that whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 must also hold. In LTL, we use the 'globally' operator to make this requirement at every point in time: globally, if prop_0 then prop_1.
This statement expresses that whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 must also hold at that same moment. In LTL, this is typically written globally (G) as 'always: if prop_0 then prop_1'.
The property states that at all times, if prop_0 holds, then prop_1 must also hold. This is a safety property and is represented using the globally (G) operator.

The formula states that globally, whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 must also hold. This is captured by the implication G (prop_0 -> prop_1), which means at every moment in time, if prop_0 is true, then prop_1 must be true as well.
Whenever prop_0 becomes true, prop_1 must also be true at the same time. This is best represented globally as: it is always the case that if prop_0 holds, then so does prop_1.

This formula states that globally, whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 must also hold at the same time. This directly encodes the implication given in the natural language statement, 'If prop_0, then prop_1.'
